preFix.title: Paper clay pressed-flower picture frame
preFix.subtitle: A decorative frame with botanical impressions from dried flowers
preFix.excerpt: A small picture frame made from paper clay with dried flower and leaf impressions pressed into the surface. The frame holds a photograph or postcard approximately 100 mm by 130 mm. No kiln required. Paper clay handles botanical impressions well because the fibre content prevents cracking at thin pressed edges.
preFix.sourceNotes: Paper clay technique derived from first principles. Botanical-impression method is a standard studio exercise adapted for air-dry clay.

content.text: A pressed-flower picture frame uses the natural texture of dried botanicals as a decorating tool. Flat flowers and leaves are pressed into a soft
slab
of
paper clay

to leave clear impressions in the surface. Paper clay is the better choice over plain air-dry clay here: the cellulose fibres bridge the fine details in botanical impressions without cracking. No kiln is required. The finished frame holds a photograph or postcard up to 100 mm by 130 mm.

items.name: Paper clay (air-dry)
items.substitution: Plain air-dry clay, though thinner impressions may crack

items.name: Card backing with easel
items.substitution: Stiff card cut to size with a fold-out stand attached

content.text: Choosing botanicals

Flat, dried specimens work best. Fern fronds, pressed pansies, individual leaves, and small flower clusters all transfer clear outlines. Three-dimensional flowers like roses do not press into clay evenly. Press flowers in a heavy book for at least two weeks before use.
Rolling and cutting the frame

Roll paper clay between the slab guides to 6 mm thickness. Cut an outer rectangle 180 mm by 210 mm. Then cut a rectangular window 90 mm by 120 mm centred in the slab, leaving a 45 mm border on all sides. The window is 10 mm smaller than the photograph on each side so the photograph sits behind the frame border.
Pressing the botanicals

Arrange the dried specimens on the frame border in a layout you are satisfied with before pressing. Position fern tips at the corners, smaller flowers along the sides. Once happy with the arrangement, press each specimen into the clay with a fingertip, using a firm even pressure. Roll over the specimens once with the rolling pin, pressing gently, to deepen the impressions. Peel each botanical away carefully. The impression remains in the clay.
Drying
Dry the frame flat on the board. Keep it flat to prevent warping. Place a sheet of thin foam or cloth under the frame to allow even airflow. The frame reaches
bone-dry
in 24-48 hours. If the frame shows signs of curling, lay a flat board on top during the last few hours of drying.
Finishing

Sand the edges lightly. Paint with acrylic paint if a coloured finish is wanted, or leave the natural clay colour. Apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish to seal the surface and deepen the impression details. Attach a card backing the size of the outer frame. Use PVA glue to fix the photograph behind the window and the card backing to the rear of the frame.
Variations
Paint the frame with a watercolour wash before varnishing to tint the botanical impressions.
Make a set of three graduating frames in different sizes using the same botanical arrangement for a wall grouping.
Use herb sprigs such as rosemary, thyme, or lavender for fragrant impressions with a culinary theme.

rows.fix: Press firmly with the rolling pin after placing each specimen. The clay should compress visibly.
rows.cause: Too little pressure when pressing the specimens

rows.fix: Paper clay handles this better than plain air-dry. Dry more slowly under a loose plastic cover. Small cracks can be mended with diluted PVA.
rows.cause: The corners of the window are stress points; clay dried too quickly
rows.problem: Frame cracks across the window opening

rows.fix: Dry on a flat porous surface and cover with a light flat board to keep the frame flat.
rows.cause: The slab dried unevenly on the top and bottom surfaces

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.1 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Make a set of three graduating frames in different sizes using the same botanical arrangement for a wall grouping.
